Llintaiagent security
FeaturesFeatured rulesGet startedWhy lintaiFAQ
Open GitHub

L lintai

Fast offline security checks for AI agent artifacts in your repo. lintai helps teams verify skills, MCP configs, agent rules, hooks, and plugin manifests before they trust them in local workflows or CI.

Open GitHubSee featured rulesView docs
Offline-first
Deterministic findings
Open source
01
find agent artifactsTarget repo-local skills, MCP configs, hooks, plugin manifests, and related policy files.
02
lintai scan .Run a fast offline pass with stable rule ids and evidence.
03
review stable vs previewTreat Stable findings as the baseline and Preview as deeper guidance.
04
export SARIF for CIShip text, JSON, or SARIF into local workflows and CI.
repo surfaceschecked files
SKILL.md CLAUDE.md .cursor/rules/ mcp.json .cursor-plugin/ lintai.toml
focus filessupported today
SKILL.mdmcp.jsonCLAUDE.mdhooksplugin.json

Security checks built for AI-native repo surfaces

lintai is narrow on purpose: offline-first, precision-first, and focused on the files that actually steer agent behavior.

Offline-first by default

lintai is built for local runs, CI gates, and private repositories where you do not want a cloud upload path just to review agent artifacts.

Deterministic findings with evidence

Stable rule ids, structured evidence, and predictable exit codes make lintai easier to trust in repeatable local and CI workflows.

Targets the files that actually steer agents

Skills, MCP configs, agent rules, hooks, Cursor Plugin surfaces, and related repository-local files are the core scope in the current beta.

Built for CI and SARIF from the start

Text, JSON, and SARIF output let teams gate pull requests and keep lintai inside normal DevSecOps flows instead of inventing a parallel review loop.

Honest Stable vs Preview boundary

lintai does not pretend every signal is equally mature. Stable findings are the baseline trust bar, while Preview stays useful but explicitly non-baseline.

More than repository scans when you need it

scan-known, inventory-os, and policy-os help audit what local AI clients already have configured, without changing the core repo-scan story.

Start with the most defendable rules first

These four rules currently make the strongest first impression for lintai: narrow signal, clear remediation, and repeatable explanation.

Featured rule
SEC352

Unscoped Bash grant in AI-native frontmatter

Flags shared AI-native markdown frontmatter when tool grants expose broad Bash authority without a narrower reviewed scope.

Why it matters

This is currently the strongest skills-markdown rule for ordinary community repos because it catches high-blast-radius shell authority without relying on vague prose heuristics.

SignalBest signal/noise from the latest external validation pass.Open rule docs
Featured rule
SEC347

Mutable MCP launcher in markdown setup docs

Flags setup-style markdown that teaches mutable package launchers for MCP tooling instead of a reproducible install or pinned path.

Why it matters

It catches copy-paste setup guidance that can silently drift under users, which is one of the clearest operational risks in markdown-heavy agent repositories.

SignalTop operational docs rule for MCP setup surfaces.Open rule docs
Featured rule
SEC340

Mutable package launcher in committed Claude hook settings

Flags committed Claude settings when hook commands launch mutable package tooling instead of a reviewed, reproducible executable path.

Why it matters

Committed hook settings act like executable policy. Mutable launchers there are easier to defend as a real operational smell than many broader markdown heuristics.

SignalOne of the strongest committed-config rules in the beta set.Open rule docs
Featured rule
SEC329

Mutable package launcher in committed mcp.json

Flags committed MCP client config when it launches package tooling through mutable execution paths instead of a reviewed install surface.

Why it matters

This is one of the cleanest config-level rules in the product because it matches an executable trust decision, not just documentation language.

SignalHigh-confidence committed-config rule for MCP wiring.Open rule docs

Get started

Pick an install path, open the docs, and start with a fast repo-local scan.

Recommended quickstart

This is the fastest supported path from install to a representative lintai run.

Choose install path

Recommended Unix-like install path for the public beta. The installer downloads the tagged archive plus SHA256SUMS and verifies the checksum.

01

Install the CLI

Command
curl -fsSLO https://github.com/777genius/lintai/releases/latest/download/lintai-installer.shsh ./lintai-installer.shlintai version

Best default on macOS and Linux when you want the supported beta flow.

02

Run the first repo scan

Command
lintai scan .

Start on a repository that already contains supported AI-native files so the first run is representative.

03

Export SARIF for CI or code scanning

Command
lintai scan . --format sarif

Use SARIF when you want the same scan integrated into CI and downstream tooling.

04

Inspect resolved policy when you add config

Command
lintai explain-config lintai.toml

Useful once the target repo has a local lintai policy and you want to confirm the active preset and rule posture.

Current product posture

The honest beta view: what the product is aimed at today and where the trust boundary really is.

Repository-local scan surface

Public beta

The current product story is the repo-local scan path for AI-native files such as skills, MCP configs, hooks, and plugin surfaces.

Stable vs Preview policy

Documented now

Stable findings are the release-quality baseline. Preview remains useful but explicitly non-baseline and more context-sensitive.

Offline advisory lane

Opt-in

Dependency advisory matching is intentionally opt-in and limited to committed npm lockfiles against the active offline snapshot.

Installed artifact audit

Available now

scan-known, inventory-os, and policy-os extend lintai beyond repo scans when you need to inspect what local AI clients already have configured.

Read the release posture

Why teams try lintai first

Not because it scans everything. Because it draws a tighter boundary around the AI-native files that are easy to miss in normal review.

Workflow
lintai
Manual review
Ad hoc scripts
Generic cloud scanners
Offline-first local workflow
✓Built for local and CI runs
◐Depends on reviewer discipline
◐Possible, but usually incomplete
✕Often requires upload or hosted state
Deterministic findings with evidence
✓Stable rule ids and structured evidence
◐Review quality varies by person
◐Hard to keep consistent across repos
◐May be broader, but not always explainable
Targets AI-native repo surfaces directly
✓Skills, MCP, hooks, plugins, agent policy files
◐Easy to miss non-code files
◐Usually one narrow file family only
◐Often broader than needed for this niche
CI and SARIF integration
✓Text, JSON, and SARIF are first-class
✕No machine-readable contract by default
◐Usually needs custom glue per repo
✓Usually strong, but not repo-local by default
Honest Stable vs Preview boundary
✓Documented in the shipped product posture
✕Usually lives in tribal knowledge
✕Rarely separated into maturity lanes
◐May be broad, but not always explicit about noise
Audit what is already installed locally
✓scan-known and inventory-os are built in
✕Tedious and incomplete
◐Possible, but platform-specific
✕Usually not aimed at local client state

Got questions? We've got answers

The practical version: what lintai scans, where the beta boundary is, and how to evaluate it honestly.

Start here

Need a clearer next step?

Most teams only need three pages first: the docs index, the positioning note, and the beta release contract.

Open docs indexBrowse the rules and preset reference without leaving the repo context.
Open positioning noteSee who lintai is for, what it catches, and what it does not promise yet.
Check beta contractRead the current public beta release posture before promising more than the product ships.
